title: Everyday Slang
description: Real, current Mandarin slang that young native speakers actually use online and with friends.
situation: Casual chats with friends, group messages, and social media - the colloquial Chinese textbooks skip.

phrases.tip: A breezy loanword from English 'hello'. Warmer and more playful than the standard 你好.
Literally means 'cow'. Say 你太牛了 (nǐ tài niú le) to call someone seriously impressive. The fuller, slightly cruder version is 牛逼 - keep it light.
Widely acceptable everywhere. 你真厉害 (nǐ zhēn lìhai) is a genuine compliment to skill or guts.

phrases.english: You got this / Go for it
phrases.tip: Literally 'add oil' (like stepping on the gas). The universal cheer for encouragement before an exam, a game, or a hard day.
Literally 'no matter'. Use it to wave off a thank-you or an apology, or to say you're fine.
Calls a person or plan trustworthy and solid. The opposite, 不靠谱 (bú kàopǔ), means flaky or sketchy.
Handy when picking a restaurant. Friendly between mates, but a flat 随便 to a host can sound a bit careless.
Punchy call to action when heading out or kicking something off. Very casual, mostly with friends.
Literally 'real or fake?'. The go-to reaction to surprising gossip or news.

phrases.english: I'm done / I give up (in disbelief)
phrases.tip: Literally 'I submit'. Half admiration, half exasperation - said when something is so over-the-top you can only throw your hands up.

phrases.english: To just chill and stop grinding
phrases.tip: Literally 'lie flat'. A young-generation buzzword for opting out of the hustle and refusing to overwork. Playfully self-aware.

phrases.english: My life is so hard right now
phrases.tip: A meme-y, half-joking groan about everyday stress. Drag out the 难 for comic effect.
Reacts to something fantastic - a dish, a song, a clutch play. Like saying 'that's next level'.
Literally 'tired to death'. The 死了 (to death) tag bolts onto loads of adjectives: 饿死了 starving, 热死了 boiling hot.
A friendly term for a male mate, with a northern er-sound flourish. Use it between guys who are close, not with strangers.
Borrowed straight from English 'bye-bye'. The casual, cheerful sign-off in chats and calls instead of the more formal 再见.
